"Unless our plans are allowed as
submitted then these tracks shall
remain as they are until the crack
of DOOM" was the ultimatum de-
livered to city councilmen this
morning by Edward ]. Cannon, di-
vision counsel of the Northern Pa
cific, after the trip over the N. P.
right of way on an inspection of
the engineering feasibility of the
grade separation plans. Just as
the party, which comprised Mr..
Cannon, City Engineer Mcintyre,
Councilmen Baldwin, Phillips,
Brown, Gray, Lambert, Funk and
Estep started, e. j. Roberts, a
heavy holder of southside realty,
arrived and announced that al.
though unlimited he thought there
would be no objection to himself
and some of his friends who are
also deeply interested by reason OF
owning effected property, accom-
panying the party.


Mr.. Cannon waxed wroth with
Mr.. Roberts and said that he had
ihNitSu CouuSiiIhCA OHly gnu Nahe
ed to know why Roberts was there.


81 want to see that such plans
only as are suitable to us are de-
cided on" was the reply.


That's just It," was Cannon's
curt rejoinder you want plans
such as are suitable to 80 out OF
a hundred people. changed to suit
YOURSELF"


Mr.. Roberts insisted that the
plans as proposed by the railroad
were entirely unsuitable to most of
the people. During the controversy
councilmen stood around i'll at ease,
waiting for settlement of the
question of whether or not Roberts
and party were to go along. Finally
Roberts said that If the councilmen
would tell him he was not wanted
.he would leave, but stated he did
Tyot think councilmen cared. Can.
N then started off ahead of the
party and Roberts followed. Coun-
Gil man Brown, who evidently did
not like Roberts manner and
course of procedure, then refused
to accompany the party, saying
